figures.caption: Figure 1. Visualization of low (high) difficulty training samples scored by MedSAM2 via derived Aleatoric Uncertainty Values (AUV). Lower AUV indicates easier-to-learn samples.
Figure 5. The correspondence between the feature vectors Z of the liver and tumors predicted by MedSAM2 and the aleatoric uncertainty values is visualized through t-SNE.
Table 3. Comparison of different uncertainty quantification methods on tumor segmentation (Dice %) in two medical applications of the LiTS dataset.

Table 1. Performance of aleatoric uncertainty-aware data filtering strategies based on multiple medical foundation models and two baseline strategies across five medical segmentation datasets. Here, ‘95%’ indicates the removal of ‘5%’ of noisy samples.
Table 2. Performance of the Dynamic Uncertainty-aware Optimization (DUO) strategy on three baseline networks.

Figure 3. Illustrative pipeline of the quantifying aleatoric uncertainty (orange part in (a)). Two applications of aleatoric uncertainty study in medical image segmentation tasks: aleatoric uncertainty-aware data filter (a) and dynamic uncertainty-aware optimization strategy (b).
